checks.question: For a message of $n = 100$ bytes on InfiniBand NDR with latency $\alpha = 1\;\mu\text{s}$ and bandwidth $50$ GB/s (so $\beta = 2 \times 10^{-11}$ s/byte), what is the total transfer time?
checks.explanation: Using $T = \alpha + \beta \cdot n = 1 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-11} \times 100 = 1 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-9} = 1.002 \times 10^{-6}$ seconds, or approximately 1.002 microseconds. The bandwidth term contributes only 2 nanoseconds -- negligible compared to the 1 microsecond latency. This message is firmly latency-dominated. The crossover point is $n^* = \alpha / \beta = 10^{-6} / (2 \times 10^{-11}) = 50{,}000$ bytes = 50 KB. Messages below this size are latency-dominated; above it, bandwidth dominates.

checks.question: A 3D torus with $k = 16$ in each dimension (4,096 nodes) has per-link bandwidth of 25 GB/s. What is its bisection bandwidth, and how does it compare to a fat tree connecting the same 4,096 nodes with 25 GB/s links?
options: Torus: 12,800 GB/s; Fat tree: 102,400 GB/s (8x more)
Torus: 512 GB/s; Fat tree: 4,096 GB/s (8x more)
They have the same bisection bandwidth of 102,400 GB/s
checks.explanation: For a $k$-ary 3D torus, bisection bandwidth $= 2 \cdot k^{n-1} \cdot b = 2 \cdot 16^2 \cdot 25 = 2 \cdot 256 \cdot 25 = 12{,}800$ GB/s. For a fat tree with $N = 4{,}096$ nodes and link bandwidth $b = 25$ GB/s, bisection bandwidth $= N \cdot b = 4{,}096 \times 25 = 102{,}400$ GB/s. The fat tree provides 8x more bisection bandwidth, which is why it dominates for all-to-all traffic patterns. However, the fat tree requires $5N/4 = 5{,}120$ switches, while the torus requires zero switches -- each node connects directly to its 6 neighbors.

checks.question: For an allreduce of a 10 MB vector across $P = 1{,}024$ processes on InfiniBand NDR ($\alpha = 1\;\mu\text{s}$, bandwidth $50$ GB/s), which algorithm is fastest?
options: All three algorithms have approximately equal cost
checks.explanation: For $P = 1{,}024$ and $n = 10^7$ bytes: Recursive Doubling: $T = \log_2(1024) \cdot 10^{-6} + \log_2(1024) \cdot 10^7 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-11} = 10 \times 10^{-6} + 10 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 10 + 2{,}000 = 2{,}010\;\mu\text{s}$. The bandwidth cost is $10 \times$ worse than optimal. Ring: $T = 2 \times 1023 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times (1023/1024) \times 10^7 \times 2 \times 10^{-11} = 2{,}046 + 399.6 \approx 2{,}446\;\mu\text{s}$. The latency cost is $1023 \times$ worse than Rabenseifner. Rabenseifner: $T = 2 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times (1023/1024) \times 10^7 \times 2 \times 10^{-11} = 20 + 399.6 \approx 420\;\mu\text{s}$. Rabenseifner wins by combining logarithmic latency (20 $\mu$s) with near-optimal bandwidth (400 $\mu$s).

checks.question: In a dragonfly topology using UGAL (Universal Globally Adaptive Load-balancing) routing, when does a packet take a non-minimal (5-hop) path instead of a minimal (3-hop) path?
options: Always, because non-minimal paths are faster due to shorter queues
When the destination is in the same group as the source
When the local queue depth on the minimal path exceeds a threshold, indicating congestion on the direct global link
Only when the network is operating above 90% utilization
checks.explanation: UGAL routing dynamically chooses between minimal (LGL, 3 hops) and non-minimal (LGLLGL, 5 hops via a random intermediate group) paths based on local queue depth. Each switch monitors its output queue lengths. If the queue for the minimal-path output port exceeds a threshold, the switch routes the packet non-minimally through a randomly chosen intermediate group. This doubles the path length but distributes traffic across global links, preventing hot spots. The beauty of this approach is that it is self-regulating: under light load, most packets take minimal paths (low latency); under adversarial traffic patterns, packets automatically spread across the network. HPE Slingshot implements UGAL decisions in silicon at per-packet granularity.
